what_it_does: Renders a fixed 1600x900 HTML composition comparing two chrome modes side by side: a document-like paper interface and a product/work scene interface, both using shared styling variables and runtime-injected icons.
observations: The page is a centered fixed-size viewport with a title, faint blueprint grid accent, bottom hatch/footer wash, and circular `V` mark.
The left `On Paper` card uses light outlined pills, tinted plates, ledger text, a stats inset, and a footer label describing document-world controls.
The right `In the Work` card uses a warm render-like gradient scene with frosted panel, dark glass menu, dark icon bar, hotspot circles, and bottom hatch.
Icons are injected with `DNA.org.icon` for ledger, calendar, plan, chat, and ledger again.
standouts: Side-by-side comparison of the same identity expressed through paper-ground and render-ground chrome.
Clear contrast between outlined light controls and dark glass/frosted controls.
Presentation is largely CSS-driven, with only icon injection handled by JavaScript.
conventions: Uses file-local CSS inside a single HTML document.
Uses CSS custom properties extensively for color, spacing, radius, and type scale.
Uses semantic class naming around visual roles such as `doc`, `prod`, `glass`, `frost`, `dplate`, and `gbar`.
Breaks responsive layout conventions by fixing the canvas at 1600x900.
concerns: The fixed viewport dimensions may not adapt to smaller browser windows.
The page depends on external CSS variables and the `DNA` runtime being present before icons can render.
The remote Google Fonts dependency affects standalone rendering.
Inline hotspot positioning is repeated directly in markup.
notes: This file is a visual substrate showing how one interface identity changes chrome behavior across document and work-scene grounds.
questions: Should this composition be viewed only as a 1600x900 presentation frame?
Are the hotspot circles decorative, interactive placeholders, or intended information targets?
Should the `V` mark represent a product identity, version marker, or annotation marker?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose matches the implementation: the page demonstrates one identity across two worlds where chrome follows the visual ground.
intention_signals: It appears intended to compare how the same design language can shift from a paper-ledger interface to an in-work product scene while preserving identity cues.
novelty: The distinct element is the paired treatment of chrome as ground-dependent: outlined paper controls on one side and dark glass/frosted controls on the other.
contribution: Contributes a file-local pattern for adaptive chrome across two substrate types, including document-world light outlined controls, product-world dark glass controls, frosted overlays, ledger language, and shared iconography.
summary_for_embedding: A fixed-size HTML/CSS presentation comparing document-ground and product-ground chrome treatments for a shared ledger-themed identity.
intention_for_embedding: Built to show how one visual identity can adapt its controls and surfaces between paper-like and work-scene contexts.
